sectionName: Biological & Biochemical Foundations of Living Systems

questions.passage: Researchers studied mitochondrial Enzyme A, a dehydrogenase that becomes less active after phosphorylation by Kinase K. In isolated mitochondria, adding pyruvate increased flux through Enzyme A, but adding a calcium-dependent phosphatase increased flux even more. A phosphomimetic mutant of Enzyme A showed low activity at all pyruvate concentrations. Immunoblotting confirmed that total Enzyme A abundance was unchanged across conditions, suggesting that the main difference was regulation of catalytic activity rather than protein expression.
questions.stem: Which condition produced the highest Enzyme A activity at 4 mM pyruvate?

explanations.A: Correct. At 4 mM pyruvate, the phosphatase-added curve is highest, indicating greater Enzyme A activity than control or the phosphomimetic mutant. This fits removal of an inhibitory phosphate group.
explanations.B: The control curve is intermediate at 4 mM and is not the highest.
explanations.C: The phosphomimetic mutant has low activity at all substrate concentrations.
explanations.D: The curves are visibly different at 4 mM.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Relative Enzyme A activity at increasing pyruvate concentrations under control, phosphatase-treated, and phosphomimetic-mutant conditions.

questions.stem: What is the most likely effect of phosphorylation on Enzyme A?

choices.A: It increases total Enzyme A expression

explanations.B: Correct. The phosphomimetic mutant, which mimics phosphorylation, has low activity even when substrate is abundant. Because total protein is unchanged, phosphorylation most likely lowers catalytic activity.
explanations.A: The passage states total Enzyme A abundance was unchanged.
explanations.C: Phosphorylation regulates the enzyme; it does not convert pyruvate into calcium.
explanations.D: Nothing in the passage suggests mitochondrial DNA destruction.

questions.stem: Why is the unchanged Enzyme A abundance important?

choices.C: It argues that activity differences are due to regulation rather than protein amount
choices.A: It proves Enzyme A is not a protein
choices.B: It shows that pyruvate is not a substrate
choices.D: It means phosphorylation cannot affect activity

explanations.C: Correct. If the amount of Enzyme A is the same across conditions, then differences in activity are more plausibly explained by changes in enzyme regulation or conformation.
explanations.A: Enzyme A is described as an enzyme and is assessed by immunoblotting, consistent with being a protein.
explanations.B: The velocity response to pyruvate supports pyruvate as a substrate or input.
explanations.D: Phosphorylation can alter activity without changing abundance.

questions.stem: Which cellular signal would most likely increase flux through Enzyme A if it activated the phosphatase?

choices.D: Increased mitochondrial calcium during muscle contraction
choices.A: Increased proteasomal degradation of Enzyme A

explanations.D: Correct. The passage describes the phosphatase as calcium-dependent and shows that phosphatase addition increases activity. More mitochondrial calcium would therefore tend to activate the phosphatase and increase flux.
explanations.A: Degradation would reduce enzyme abundance rather than explain the unchanged-protein pattern.
explanations.B: Loss of pyruvate transport would reduce substrate availability and decrease flux.
explanations.C: Permanent substrate-binding inhibition would reduce activity rather than increase it.

questions.stem: Which result would most directly show that phosphorylation changes Vmax rather than substrate affinity only?

choices.A: The phosphomimetic mutant has a lower maximal activity even at saturating pyruvate
choices.B: The curves differ only at the lowest pyruvate concentration
choices.C: All curves reach the same plateau at high pyruvate
choices.D: Total Enzyme A mRNA rises after phosphatase treatment

explanations.A: Correct. A lower plateau at saturating substrate indicates a reduced Vmax. The phosphomimetic curve stays low even at high pyruvate, which is consistent with reduced catalytic capacity.
explanations.B: A difference only at low substrate would point more toward affinity changes.
explanations.C: The same plateau would argue against a Vmax difference.
explanations.D: mRNA abundance would address expression, not kinetic Vmax.

questions.passage: A biochemistry group examined phosphofructokinase-1 (PFK-1) activity in liver extracts. ATP and citrate decreased PFK-1 activity, while AMP and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate increased activity. When ATP was high and AMP was low, glycolytic flux fell. When AMP rose after simulated exercise, glycolytic flux increased even though ATP was still present. The investigators concluded that PFK-1 integrates energy status and carbohydrate availability to regulate glycolysis.
questions.stem: Which condition shows the highest PFK-1 activity?

explanations.B: Correct. The combined AMP and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate condition has the tallest bar at 96%, indicating the strongest activation among the tested conditions.
explanations.A: High ATP lowers activity relative to activating conditions.
explanations.C: ATP plus citrate gives the lowest activity in the figure.
explanations.D: High AMP activates PFK-1, but not as strongly as AMP plus fructose-2,6-bisphosphate.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Relative PFK-1 activity in liver extracts exposed to different allosteric regulators.

questions.stem: Why does high ATP inhibit PFK-1?

choices.C: ATP signals sufficient cellular energy and reduces glycolytic flux
choices.A: ATP is the substrate for DNA ligase only
choices.D: ATP prevents glucose from entering every cell

explanations.C: Correct. High ATP indicates that cellular energy charge is high, so slowing glycolysis conserves glucose and prevents unnecessary ATP production.
explanations.A: ATP has many roles and is also a substrate/regulator in metabolism; this choice is too narrow.
explanations.B: The passage describes reversible allosteric regulation, not enzyme destruction.
explanations.D: ATP does not universally prevent glucose entry.

questions.stem: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is best described as what in this passage?

choices.B: A product of the TCA cycle

explanations.D: Correct. The figure shows high PFK-1 activity when fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is high, and the passage states that it increases activity. This identifies it as an allosteric activator.
explanations.A: The passage focuses on PFK-1, not competitive inhibition of hexokinase.
explanations.B: Fructose-2,6-bisphosphate is not presented as a TCA-cycle product.
explanations.C: It is a regulatory sugar phosphate, not a membrane lipid.

questions.stem: What would be expected during simulated exercise when AMP rises?

explanations.A: Correct. AMP is a low-energy signal that activates PFK-1. The passage says glycolytic flux increased when AMP rose, even in the presence of ATP.
explanations.B: The passage reports increased rather than decreased flux.
explanations.C: Citrate is inhibitory, but the relevant exercise signal here is increased AMP.
explanations.D: The findings demonstrate allosteric regulation rather than loss of it.

questions.stem: Which conclusion best integrates the data?

choices.B: PFK-1 acts as a control point that responds to both energy charge and regulatory metabolites
choices.A: PFK-1 activity depends only on enzyme abundance
choices.C: ATP and AMP affect PFK-1 in the same direction
choices.D: Citrate is the strongest activator of glycolysis

explanations.B: Correct. The passage shows opposite effects of high-energy and low-energy signals, plus regulation by citrate and fructose-2,6-bisphosphate. That makes PFK-1 a major integrative regulatory step.
explanations.A: The passage manipulates allosteric regulators, not enzyme amount.
explanations.C: ATP inhibits while AMP activates, so they do not act in the same direction.
explanations.D: Citrate decreases activity in the figure.
